mappings.rationaleAI: Jung's concepts of introversion and extroversion constitute foundational personality typology (A.4), his collective unconscious and archetypes extend psychoanalytic personality theory beyond Freud (A.2), and individuation as the process of psychological self-realization directly addresses development of self-awareness (C.1).

Freudian theories of superego and unconscious guilt relate to psychoanalytic personality structure (A.2), guilt as a force distorting reason and undermining psychological well-being constitutes a psychological stress (C.2), and pathological guilt that undermines functioning connects to defense mechanisms (C.3) and mental disorder (D.1).

Neuroscience's account of how brain structure gives rise to selfhood and moral responsibility bears on physiological theories of personality (A.1) and subjective self-awareness (C.1), while implications for treating neurological and psychiatric conditions connect to major categories and causation of mental disorders (D.1, D.3).

Freud's Group Psychology and the Analysis of the Ego directly engages psychoanalytic personality theory including the conflict between permitted and repressed ideas within the psyche (A.2), and the mechanisms of repression as defense mechanisms for coping with internal and external demands (C.3).

Examination of consciousness, subjective experience, and Freud's framework of the subconscious relates to psychoanalytic personality structure (A.2), while exploration of self-awareness and qualia directly addresses the subjective aspect of personality and development of awareness of self (C.1).

The development and critique of IQ testing from Binet onward, including factor analysis of cognitive abilities, nature-versus-nurture debates, twin studies, and cultural bias in assessment, directly illustrates methods of psychological assessment (B.1) and the evaluation of assessment techniques for reliability and validity (B.2).

Competing accounts of how mind relates to body — including identity theory, functionalism, and eliminative materialism — bear directly on physiological theories of personality (A.1), while the philosophical treatment of consciousness, free will, and personal identity addresses the subjective aspect of personality and self-awareness (C.1).

Drug use as a means of altering perception and mood illustrates coping responses to environmental strain (C.3), the cycle of pleasure and self-destruction exemplifies deprivation of accustomed gratification as a psychological stress (C.2), and substance-related disorders constitute a recognized category of mental disorder (D.1).

Woolf's interweaving of sanity and insanity through Clarissa Dalloway and shell-shocked veteran Septimus Smith substantively explores the subjective experience of selfhood (C.1) and portrays posttraumatic stress disorder as a persisting disturbance of personality integration (D.1).

Solitude's role in self-knowledge, Arendt's productive inner dialogue through chosen aloneness, and the spectrum from enforced isolation to voluntary contemplation relate to the integration of the person as a whole and self-reflection (A).
